title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about migration and the experience of building a new cultural identity. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Between Two Shores
content.subtitle: An article about migration, belonging, and cultural identity.
content.text:
The experience of migration is rarely a single, definitive event. It is, rather, a process that begins long before departure and continues long after arrival — a process of constant negotiation between the person one was and the person one is becoming. [31] For many migrants, the most challenging aspect is not the practical business of establishing themselves in a new country, but the subtler disorientation that comes from inhabiting two cultural worlds simultaneously, belonging fully to neither. [/31]

This phenomenon has been described in various ways — as living between cultures, as occupying a 'third space', as having a hyphenated identity. None of these terms fully captures the experience, partly because it varies so enormously depending on the circumstances of migration, the age at which it occurs, and the degree of difference between origin and destination cultures. [32] A child who arrives at the age of eight will typically integrate through school, friendships, and language in a way that is largely invisible; an adult who arrives at fifty may find the process far more deliberate and effortful, never quite losing the sense of being a guest in someone else's country. [/32]

The second generation — those born in a country to migrant parents — often experience a particular version of this tension. [33] They are shaped by two cultures: the domestic world of parents who retained the language, food, and customs of their origin country, and the external world of school, media, and peers. Navigating between these worlds can be a source of richness, but it can also generate a kind of chronic low-level exhaustion — the constant effort of code-switching, of adjusting tone, vocabulary, and behaviour depending on context. [/33]

Public debates about migration frequently focus on economic contributions and national security, framing the issue as a question of costs and benefits. [34] What this framing consistently omits is the subjective dimension: the internal landscape of the migrant themselves, their grief, their ambition, their humour, and their complex attachment to multiple places at once. Literature and film have sometimes succeeded where policy language has failed, giving form and particularity to lives that are easily reduced to statistics. [/34]

In recent decades, there has been growing academic interest in the concept of 'transnationalism' — the idea that migrants do not simply leave one country and join another, but maintain meaningful ties to both simultaneously. [35] Modern communication technologies have made this easier: a person can now follow the news from their city of origin in real time, maintain close relationships across continents, and participate in the cultural and even political life of a country they have not physically inhabited for decades. [/35]

None of this makes migration an easy experience. But it does suggest that the old binary model — in which a migrant either assimilates or remains a foreigner — has outlived its usefulness. [36] Identity, it turns out, is not a fixed destination but a continuous journey, one that migration intensifies without necessarily resolving. [/36]

questions.question: According to the author, the most difficult challenge for many migrants is
options: A) finding suitable employment in their new country.
B) learning to speak the language of their host country.
C) feeling that they do not fully belong to either of their two cultures.
D) maintaining relationships with people in their country of origin.
questions.explanation: The author states the greatest challenge is 'the subtler disorientation that comes from inhabiting two cultural worlds simultaneously, belonging fully to neither'.

questions.question: The contrast between children and adults arriving in a new country illustrates that
options: A) children always find migration easier than adults.
B) the ease of integration depends on a range of factors, including age.
C) adults who migrate are less motivated to integrate than children.
D) children who migrate often struggle more at school than at home.
questions.explanation: The examples are given to support the claim that the experience 'varies so enormously depending on the circumstances of migration, the age at which it occurs', among other factors.

questions.question: The author uses the phrase 'chronic low-level exhaustion' to describe
options: A) the physical tiredness caused by working in a second language.
B) the effort required to constantly adapt one's behaviour between two cultures.
C) the anxiety that second-generation migrants feel about their future.
D) the difficulty of maintaining relationships with parents who hold different values.
questions.explanation: The exhaustion is directly linked to 'the constant effort of code-switching, of adjusting tone, vocabulary, and behaviour depending on context'.

questions.question: The author criticises public debates about migration for
options: A) focusing too heavily on cultural rather than economic factors.
B) ignoring the personal and emotional experiences of migrants themselves.
C) relying too much on literature and film to inform policy.
D) failing to address the security concerns of host communities.
questions.explanation: The author says public debate omits 'the subjective dimension: the internal landscape of the migrant themselves', including emotions and personal attachments.

questions.question: The concept of 'transnationalism' suggests that migrants
options: A) prefer to return to their home country once they have saved enough money.
B) are unable to form genuine attachments to their host country.
C) often keep active connections with both their origin and host countries.
D) only use technology to maintain contact with family members.
questions.explanation: Transnationalism describes migrants who 'maintain meaningful ties to both simultaneously', engaging with the cultural and political life of both countries.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author argues that
options: A) migrants should ultimately choose one culture over the other.
B) migration is an experience that eventually becomes straightforward.
C) the traditional view of migrants as either assimilated or foreign is outdated.
D) identity is primarily shaped by the country in which a person is born.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly states that 'the old binary model — in which a migrant either assimilates or remains a foreigner — has outlived its usefulness'.
